text: As wireless data growth continues to outpace network capacity, Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) are increasingly exploring means of managing congestion. One of the most widely adopted practices is pushing data traffic from cellular to WiFi, referred to as inter radio access technology (RAT) offloading. While offloading the increasingly congested cellular network does provide some benefits, there is limited understanding of the long term implications of such practices and their overall system performance. In this work, we develop a general yet tractable model comprising $M$ different RATs, each having up to $K$ different tiers of access points (APs). Each tier is characterized by a unique transmit power, path loss exponent, deployment density, and bandwidth. We model each class of APs using an independent Poisson point process (PPP) and mobile user locations using another independent PPP. Furthermore, all channels are characterized by i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. We derive the distribution of rates in the network and evaluate a weighted association strategy with tunable weights that can be optimized to achieve various objectives. We find that the optimum fraction of traffic to be offloaded in order to maximize $\SINR$ coverage is not necessarily the same as the optimum fraction of traffic to be offloaded in order to maximize the rate coverage, i.e., the fraction of users that achieve a given rate.
